name: La Valderia - circular from La Banenza to Castrocalbon and Castrocontrigo
name_original: La Valdería – circular desde La Bañeza a Castrocalbón y Castrocontrigo
region_intro: Touring cycling around La Bañeza offers diverse landscapes, from riverside forests to open fields, situated in the southern part of León, Spain. The region is characterized by its varied terrain, including areas along the Órbigo river and historical routes like the Vía de la Plata. These features provide a range of physical environments suitable for touring cyclists. The area's network of routes often features well-paved surfaces, making it accessible for different cycling preferences. Best touring cycling routes around La Bañeza The most popular touring cycling route is To the Sierra de casas Viejas from La Bañeza, a 21.1 miles (33.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 53 minutes to complete, featuring moderate elevation gain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is La Valderia - circular from La Banenza to Castrocalbon and Castrocontrigo, a moderate 42.3 miles (68.0 km) path. This route explores the Valdería region, passing through towns like Castrocalbón and Castrocontrigo. Local touring cyclists also love the Vía de la Plata and Páramo Leones Loop, a 46.1 miles (74.2 km) trail leading through historical landscapes and open plains, often completed in about 4 hours 45 minutes. Touring cycling around La Bañeza is defined by historical routes, riverside forests, and agricultural fields, offering a blend of cultural heritage and natural settings. The network of routes provides options for different ability levels, from moderate to more challenging rides. The routes in La Bañeza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 70 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore La Bañeza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around La Bañeza?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around La Bañeza, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes explore the diverse landscapes of the region, from riverside paths to historical trails.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling around La Bañeza?
A: Cycling around La Bañeza offers a blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage. You'll encounter diverse landscapes including riverside forests, open agricultural fields, and historical routes like the Vía de la Plata. The region also features areas along the Órbigo river and the distinct terrains of Valduerna and Valdería.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in La Bañeza?
A: Yes, La Bañeza offers several routes suitable for beginners and families. There are 4 easy routes available, providing gentler terrain and shorter distances to enjoy the scenery at a relaxed pace. These routes are generally well-paved and accessible.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, La Bañeza has 2 difficult touring cycling routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24310822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of La Bañeza – Church of Santa Elena de Jamuz loop from La Bañeza&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 54.4 km and features significant elevation gain, providing a demanding ride through varied terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in La Bañeza?
A: The best time for touring cycling in La Bañeza is typically during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these seasons, the weather is generally mild and pleasant, with comfortable temperatures for cycling and less extreme heat than in summer.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available in La Bañeza?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around La Bañeza are circular, allowing you to start and end your journey in the same location. An example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035221772/la-valderia-circular-from-la-banenza-to-castrocalbon-and-castrocontrigo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Valderia - circular from La Banenza to Castrocalbon and Castrocontrigo&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a 68 km loop through the Valdería region.

Q: What historical or cultural sites can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: La Bañeza is rich in history. Many routes follow or cross the ancient Vía de la Plata, a significant Roman road and pilgrimage route. You can also encounter historical landmarks within the city, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5460401" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of La Bañeza&lt;/a&gt;, and charming town centers like Alija del Infantado. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032787063/via-de-la-plata-and-paramo-leones-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata and Páramo Leones Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights these historical landscapes.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in La Bañeza?
A: The touring cycling routes in La Bañeza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 8 reviews. Cyclists often praise the blend of cultural heritage and natural settings, the varied terrain, and the well-maintained surfaces that make the routes accessible and enjoyable.

Q: Are there any specific points of interest or landmarks to look out for?
A: Absolutely! Beyond the routes themselves, you can explore several points of interest. In La Bañeza, don't miss the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5460401" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5823160" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Town Hall and Clock Tower&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3536793" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Street Art and Monument to Santiago&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, you might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5470465" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Eria Bridge in Castrocalbón&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5510280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gothic Arch of the Palace of Castrocalbón&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on touring cycling routes in La Bañeza?
A: The elevation gain varies significantly depending on the route. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1384000820/to-the-sierra-de-casas-viejas-from-la-baneza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;To the Sierra de casas Viejas from La Bañeza&lt;/a&gt; route has an elevation gain of approximately 365 meters, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032787063/via-de-la-plata-and-paramo-leones-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata and Páramo Leones Loop&lt;/a&gt; has a more gentle gain of about 197 meters. Routes are categorized by difficulty, which often correlates with elevation.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through towns with cafes or amenities?
A: Many routes are designed to pass through or near charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and other amenities. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035221772/la-valderia-circular-from-la-banenza-to-castrocalbon-and-castrocontrigo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Valderia circular route&lt;/a&gt; passes through towns like Castrocalbón and Castrocontrigo, offering opportunities for breaks and refreshments.

Q: Is public transport available to access the cycling routes around La Bañeza?
A: La Bañeza is a well-connected town, and while specific public transport links directly to trailheads might vary, the town itself serves as a central hub. You can typically reach La Bañeza by bus from larger cities in the León province, and from there, many routes are accessible directly from the town center or a short ride away.
tips: Important town in Leon with a rich architectural heritage that surprises many of its visitors. I love its main square and its railway infrastructure, which I think give it all its character, but there are also buildings like the churches of San Salvador or Santa María, or even the Town Hall, which are quite impressive.

There are several theories about the peculiar name of this Castro: some suggest that it comes from the lime that was manufactured here; others if it was founded by a member of the Calvo Roman family; It is also thought that it may be due to the oak-based charcoal that was produced in its vicinity, and there are those who think that the origin of the name is, on the contrary, Mozarabic.

Although the current name of this town is much clearer than that of its neighbor Castrocalbón, it is interesting to know that the origin of Castrocontrigo is in the primitive city of Aria, which was the one that gave its name to the Aria/Eria river, which crosses the municipality. , and in turn to the entire Valle del Aria, which ended up being known as Valdería.
Small sculpture that pays homage to the apostle and the pilgrims in this city on the Camino.
